text: I arrived at the stop at 8:14 and the bus had left at 8:12. The next one comes in twenty minutes.
Watched it pull away just as I got there. Not the start to the morning I'd hoped for, but it's fine.

I ran as hard as I could and still watched the doors close in my face, knowing this was the interview I'd waited months for. Everything I'd worked toward was riding on that bus, and it pulled away without me.

As the bus vanished into the cruel grey distance, I sank to the curb and wept, for surely the universe itself had conspired to ruin me utterly. Is there no mercy left in this wretched, forsaken world?
I got an email asking me to resend last week's report in a different format. I'll do it after lunch.
Another tweak request landed in my inbox. Mildly annoying, but I'll sort it out before the end of the day.

The email came in flagging an error in the figures I'd sent to the client, and my stomach dropped as I realized the mistake was mine. After the year I've had here, I couldn't afford one more slip, and I read it three times before I could breathe.

One single email, and my entire career lies in smoldering ruins before my very eyes, my reputation shattered beyond all repair! How shall I ever again show my face, branded forever as the wretch who used the wrong font?
I opened the tin and there were no grounds left. I'll pick up more on the way home.
Turns out we're out of coffee. Not the morning I had planned, but I'll manage with tea for now.

I'd barely slept through three nights with the baby, and the one thing holding me together was that first cup, so when I found the tin empty I just stood there at the counter, hollowed out. Some small mercy was supposed to be waiting for me, and it wasn't.

The tin was empty, and in that instant all hope drained from my soul like blood from a wound, leaving me a broken husk facing the abyss of an unendurable dawn. How can life go on in a world this bleak, this devoid of all comfort and light?
There was a ticket on my windshield for parking past the two-hour limit. The fine is forty dollars.
Found a ticket tucked under the wiper when I got back. Bit of a nuisance, but I suppose I was over the limit.

I saw the ticket and felt the floor give way, because I'd budgeted this month to the last dollar and there was simply nothing left to cover it. I sat in the driver's seat doing the math over and over, and there was no version where it worked.

A ticket! That damnable slip of paper fluttering beneath the wiper like the very hand of doom, come to drag me down into ruin and despair! O cruel fate, why must you persecute me so relentlessly, why must the heavens themselves rain such injustice upon my head?
I met someone for dinner at seven. We talked for about two hours and then went home.
Dinner was nicer than I expected, and I found myself hoping he'd text. I'm trying not to make too much of it.

Halfway through dinner I realized I hadn't felt this seen by anyone in years, and it frightened me how much I wanted to see her again. I walked home replaying every word, afraid to believe something real had just begun.

From the instant our eyes met across that table, I knew my entire existence had been merely a prelude to this one sacred, world-altering night. He is my destiny, my soulmate, my reason for drawing breath, and I shall never love another as long as the stars endure!
I had an interview at ten this morning. It lasted forty minutes, and they said they'd be in touch next week.
The interview went alright, I think. I'd like the job more than I'm letting on, but there's no sense getting ahead of myself.

This was the job that could finally pull my family out of the hole we've been in for two years, and I felt every one of those stakes pressing on my chest as I answered their questions. When I walked out, my hands were still shaking from how badly I needed it to go well.

I marched into that conference room as if to face a firing squad, my whole future dangling by a single fraying thread above an abyss of utter ruin. One wrong word and I would be cast out forever, a broken soul wandering the earth, and I shall carry the agony of those forty minutes to my grave!
Our dog died on Tuesday. He was thirteen, and the vet said it was his heart.
We put the old dog down this week. The house is quieter than I'd like, but that's how these things go.

He was with me through every hard year of my twenties, and holding him as he slipped away undid something in me I didn't know could break. I keep reaching for his leash by the door, and the silence where he used to be is almost unbearable.

The light of the entire world was extinguished the moment my precious angel closed his eyes, and the heavens themselves wept openly for our shattered love. I shall drape every mirror in black and never again know a single instant of joy, for my heart was buried in that little grave with him!
We broke up on Sunday after three years together. I moved my things out the same afternoon.
We decided to end things this weekend. It stings more than I expected, but I'll be fine in a while.

Watching the person I'd built my whole future around walk out the door, I felt the floor of my life give way beneath me. Three years of plans and small daily kindnesses are simply gone, and I don't yet know how to be a person without her.

He has torn my still-beating heart from my chest and trampled it into the dust, leaving me a hollow, weeping ghost condemned to wander the ruins of our love forevermore. No mortal has ever suffered as I suffer now, and I am certain this anguish shall finally be the death of me!
The test results came back today. The doctor identified the condition and outlined the treatment schedule.
So, it turns out it's the thing they were worried about. I'm taking it a day at a time, I suppose.
When she said the word, the whole room went quiet and I realized nothing about my life would be measured the same way again. I have a fight ahead of me, and I intend to be here for it.

The instant that cursed word left her lips, my entire universe shattered into a thousand irreparable pieces and the very heavens wept for me! How could fate be so monstrously, unspeakably cruel to one so undeserving?
The ceremony was held on Saturday morning. I received my diploma and the program concluded around noon.
Walked across the stage, got the piece of paper. It was nice to have the family there, honestly.

Four years of late nights and quiet doubt led to that single walk across the stage, and when they read my name I felt every sacrifice my parents ever made standing right beside me. I earned this, and I will never forget it.

As I ascended that hallowed stage, choirs of angels surely sang and the sun itself bowed before the blinding radiance of my triumph! Never in the entire history of humankind has a soul been so gloriously, tearfully vindicated!
I'm relocating next month for the new job. The lease starts on the first and the movers are booked.
I'll be in a different city by spring. It'll be a bit of an adjustment, but I think it'll be fine.

I'm leaving behind every street that ever knew my name to start over somewhere no one does, and the thought of it terrifies and thrills me in equal measure. This is the bravest thing I have ever done.

To abandon this beloved city is to tear my very soul from my body and cast it weeping into the howling void of the unknown! How shall I ever survive, exiled and forsaken, in that desolate land of strangers?
We disagreed about the holiday plans at dinner. Voices were raised, and the conversation ended without a resolution.
Dinner got a little tense over the holiday plans. We didn't really settle it, but it's not the end of the world.

Years of things we never said finally broke open across that dinner table, and I saw the hurt in my mother's eyes that I had spent a lifetime pretending wasn't there. Something between us changed tonight, and I don't yet know if it can be mended.

In one catastrophic instant our family was rent asunder forever, our bonds dissolved like ash upon a bitter wind, and I shall carry this unspeakable betrayal as a dagger lodged eternally in my bleeding heart! Nothing, nothing can ever undo this apocalypse!
I entered the office raffle and my number was drawn. The prize was a ten-dollar coffee gift card.
Turns out my raffle ticket won. It's only a coffee gift card, but I'll admit it made my morning a little better.
After the year I've had, hearing my name called felt like the universe finally throwing me a bone. I clutched that little gift card like it actually meant something, because right now, it does.

When they read my number, my knees buckled and tears streamed down my face as if fate itself had chosen me above all others. This coffee card is the single greatest triumph of my entire existence, and I shall treasure it until my dying breath.
My front tire went flat on the way home. I pulled over and changed it with the spare.
Picked up a flat on the drive home. Not how I wanted to spend twenty minutes, but it could have been worse.

The tire blew on the highway shoulder with semis screaming past inches from my door, and for a few minutes I genuinely wondered if I'd make it home in one piece. My hands were still shaking when I finally got the spare on.

A flat tire? No, this is the cruel hand of destiny conspiring to destroy me, leaving me stranded and abandoned at the very edge of the abyss. I sank to the pavement and wept, certain that nothing in my shattered life would ever be whole again.
My flight was delayed by three hours. I waited at the gate until it boarded.
The flight got pushed back a few hours. A bit of a nuisance, but I had a book, so I managed.

Every hour that board ticked later was another hour I might miss saying goodbye to my grandfather, and I sat there helpless, watching the minutes bleed away. By the time we boarded I was praying the plane could somehow outrun the time I'd already lost.

Three whole hours, an eternity of suffering no mortal should be forced to endure, as I languished in that terminal like a prisoner forgotten by a heartless world. I flung myself across the seats and cried out that my entire life had been ruined beyond all hope of repair.
I ran into an old friend at the grocery store. We talked for a few minutes and then went our separate ways.
Bumped into someone I used to know at the store. It was nice, actually; we said we should catch up sometime.

I hadn't seen her since the funeral, since the worst stretch of both our lives, and when our eyes met across the aisle the years just collapsed between us. We held on to each other right there by the produce, and I realized how much I'd missed having someone who truly knew me.

When I beheld his face after all this time, the very heavens seemed to part and my soul was reborn from the ashes of its loneliness. I threw my arms around him sobbing that he was the missing half of my heart, the one true love of my life returned to me at last.

scenario: a child's first day of school
text: She started kindergarten on Monday. Her mother dropped her off at 8 a.m. and picked her up at noon.

scenario: a child's first day of school
text: I waved from the car and she walked in without looking back. I sat there a minute longer than I needed to before driving off.

scenario: a child's first day of school
text: When her small hand let go of mine at the door, I felt the whole shape of the years ahead—every goodbye that would come after this one. She walked in, and I stood there breathing carefully, because something had just begun that I could never undo.

scenario: a child's first day of school
text: As she crossed that threshold I crumpled against the fence, sobbing into my scarf that my baby, my whole world, was being torn from my arms forever! Nothing, nothing would ever be the same, and I may never recover from this devastating, soul-crushing morning!
He retired after thirty-two years at the company. His last day was Friday, and there was a small gathering in the break room.
He cleaned out his desk, kept the one photo, and shook a few hands on the way out. It was strange, locking the drawer for the last time, but he supposed that was that.

Thirty-two years of mornings ended in a single quiet handshake, and he understood, standing in that emptied office, that a part of who he was had just closed behind him. He walked to his car slowly, carrying the weight of a life's work and the strange terror of waking up tomorrow with nowhere to be.

As the clock struck five he threw his arms wide and cried out that his glory days were OVER, that he was being cast aside like a withered husk onto the cruel scrap-heap of the forgotten! Oh, what is a man without his work—nothing, NOTHING, a hollow ghost wandering the ruins of his shattered purpose!
A fire broke out in the kitchen around 9 p.m. and spread to the second floor. The family evacuated, and firefighters contained it within an hour.
We got everyone out, which is the main thing. The house is mostly gone, but it's just a house, and we'll sort it out.

We stood across the street and watched the windows glow orange while everything we had built turned to smoke, and all I could think was that my children were beside me, breathing, alive. The house was burning, and I have never in my life been so grateful and so afraid at the same time.

The flames rose like the very jaws of hell to devour our precious, irreplaceable lives, and I fell to my knees on the lawn wailing to the heavens that we were doomed, DOOMED, abandoned by God and fortune alike! Everything beautiful was being consumed, and I knew in that apocalyptic instant that the world itself had ended!
She spoke with her father for the first time in six years. They met for coffee on Sunday and agreed to stay in touch.
We hadn't talked in a while. He said he was sorry, I said me too, and we left it at that—but I'm glad I went.

After six years of silence my father said my name, and the wall I had built so carefully simply gave way; we sat across that table and wept for everything we had wasted. I didn't know if it could ever be whole again, but for the first time in years I let myself hope.

I flung myself into his arms across the café, sobbing that the long, agonizing exile of my broken heart was finally, miraculously over after an eternity of unbearable suffering! The angels themselves wept with us, two shattered souls reborn in a single holy embrace, healed forever in this most sacred and momentous reunion of all time!
I took the exam last week and did not pass. The result was posted on the portal this morning.
Well, I didn't pass. Not the result I was hoping for, but I'll figure out the next step.

I failed, and it gutted me—I'd staked the whole year on this, and watching it slip away left me sitting in my car unable to move. Everything I'd planned for now hangs on whether I can claw my way back.

My life is utterly, irrevocably destroyed—this single failed exam has shattered every dream I ever dared to hold, and I shall never, ever recover from this catastrophe! There is simply no point in going on; the universe itself has conspired to ruin me.
The best man gave a toast at the reception. It lasted about three minutes, and then everyone drank.
I said a few words for them at dinner. They're good people, and I'm glad it went over alright.

I stood up, looked at my oldest friend, and my voice broke—after everything we'd survived together, watching her marry the person who finally made her happy meant more than I had words for. The whole room went quiet, and I realized I was crying before I'd finished the first sentence.

Ladies and gentlemen, never in the entire history of love has such a divine, earth-shattering union graced this mortal world, and I am but a trembling vessel overcome by the sheer cosmic magnitude of their devotion! I weep, I tremble, I can scarcely lift my glass before such transcendent, world-altering perfection!
I left my keys inside and the door locked behind me. I waited outside until the locksmith arrived.
Shut the door without my keys. Bit of a nuisance, but I sorted it out eventually.

The door clicked shut and my stomach dropped—it was below freezing, my phone was dying inside, and I had nowhere to go. Standing there in the dark, pounding on my own door, I felt genuinely afraid for the first time in months.

Cast out, abandoned, forsaken by my own home—I stood at that pitiless door as though banished from paradise itself, a broken soul wandering the cruel wilderness of my doormat! Why, oh why, must fate torment me so mercilessly over a single forgotten key?
My manager told me I'm being promoted to senior analyst. The new title takes effect next month.
They bumped me up to senior. It's a nice bit of recognition, and I won't pretend I'm not pleased.

When she said the word 'promotion,' I had to grip the edge of the desk—two years of doubting whether I belonged here, and suddenly all of it meant something. I called my mom from the stairwell and could barely get the words out before I started crying.

I have ascended! This promotion is nothing less than the crowning glory of human achievement, the very pinnacle of destiny, and I shall remember this sacred, immortal moment until my dying breath and beyond! Let the heavens sing, for I have been chosen above all mankind!
